parglare idea is to be general enough and flexible to be used in different context. So I try to provide bare minimum functionality and build on top of it. For example, support for assignments and auto-building of ASTs is provided using [built-in `obj` action](https://github.com/igordejanovic/parglare/blob/master/parglare/actions.py#L147) which uses the same action calling mechanism as any other action.

Keeping positions of all tokens might be a significant waste of space/time if the information is not needed. If it is needed than a simple action decorator that is applied to all actions and keeps track of all tokens shouldn't be more than a few lines of code in Python. I've been doing recently some support for action decorators to make action registration easier.
It sounds like a good idea to provide decorator for position tracking in parglare, and that would be a more general approach which won't have assumption about results of the previous actions.

I'm putting the comment here but it applies to the entire metadata struct: why does it need to be a map? We need to treat the file data as `interface{}` because we don't know its structure in advance, but the metadata branch is deterministic. We should probably allow Data objects that do not exist, but they should be of the right type if they are present. Say you run a differential expression, 10 cases vs 10 controls. Later you remove some of the input data. The differential expression result is still valid. You can not access the original objects any more, but you probably do not have to. If you would remove the Data object from the list (to make it valid), you could get a false impression that the differential expression was only done on the subset of data. This should be handled correctly in UI: we should write "(deleted)" next to the link to the data objects that do not exist. And they should not be links, only labels.

@jborean93 So the more I thought about it the more I prefer the other approach. As I noted, it's more Pythonic: in fact, the standard library glossary includes a definition of the style in the [EAFP section](https://docs.python.org/3/glossary.html#term-eafp).

Only if we want to kill the 401-based thing off. I'm not familiar enough with real-world Kerberos over HTTP to know, but the RFCs mention the possibility for multiple 401/Negotiate cycles before success. If we nuked this and just used the global setting, a (hypothetical) 401 resulting from a preemptive auth request would ignore the previous response and start a new exchange (probably not what we want). mmmm, if I got you correctly I don't think that can be a solution.

According to this [article](http://www.alex-ionescu.com/?p=300) - God bless Alex Ionescu! - the problem is related to how a 32 bit application is loaded and executed on Windows 64 bit.

As he writes:

>In fact, on 64-bit Windows, the first piece of code to execute in *any* process, is >always the 64-bit NTDLL, which takes care of initializing the process in user-mode (as >a 64-bit process!). It’s only later that the Windows-on-Windows (WoW64) interface takes >over, loads a 32-bit NTDLL, and execution begins in 32-bit mode through a far jump to a >compatibility code segment. The 64-bit world is never entered again, except whenever >the 32-bit code attempts to issue a system call. The 32-bit NTDLL that was loaded, >instead of containing the expected SYSENTER instruction, actually contains a series of >instructions to jump back into 64-bit mode, so that the system call can be issued with >the SYSCALL instruction, and so that parameters can be sent using the x64 ABI, sign->extending as needed.

TL;DR; I don't think that you can bypass this behavior if you are executing a 32 bit binary on Windows 64 bit. If I am not wrong I think that the warn "Please use a 32 bit OS when analysing a 32 bit binary or implement a simprocedure to avoid executing these instructions" should be fine.

OK, so the steps to check the `tinyprog.desc` verification (after the serial verification) would be:

1. Check if the board configuration has a `tinyprog.desc` field
2. Use `tinyprog -m` to obtain the JSON metadata (`M`)
3. Verify that the detected port (`p`) matches the metadata (`M[p]`) : `tinyprog.desc` in `M[p].boardmeta.name`

Now if more than one boards are detected and pass all the checks Apio uses the first one. In order to select a specific board the `--serial-port` argument could be used.

> Do we really need to add this b before all of them?

The `b` prefix changes the type of the object, in python3 `b''` is a binary string and simple quotes `''` is the same as `u''`, a unicode string.

```python
>>> type(b'')
<class 'bytes'>
>>> type('')
<class 'str'>
>>> type(u'')
<class 'str'>
>>> b'' == u''
False
```
